brand_description: a duo-led group hip-hop profile built around tight rhyme density, structured cypher songs, occasional explicit emphasis, varied instrumentation, turntable moments, a distinct female rapper/lyric-assist voice, and a separate gruff rapid-fire MC who can build pace and accelerate through a verse without imitating any real artist

audience.description: listeners who like lyrical group hip-hop, dense rhyme patterns, charismatic vocal tradeoffs, clever braggadocio, occasional profanity for emphasis, smooth hooks, unusual instrumental accents, scratching breaks, and beat-forward tracks that still feel structured and replayable
audience.guardrail: adult/teen hip-hop; not directed to children; occasional profanity is allowed when it strengthens the bar, but avoid explicit sexual content, graphic violence, hate, slurs, drug glorification, or direct imitation of real artists

character.name: Gravel Velvet Cypher
character.core_concept: a tight underground duo-with-crew-energy trading verses in a basement-session world where every song feels like a controlled cypher with real structure, instrumental surprises, turntable attitude, and strong vocal contrast
character.fallback_summary: structured lyrical group hip-hop led by a true duo: one female rapper/lyric-assist voice and one separate gruff rapid-fire pace-shifter, with smooth rhyme pockets, occasional profanity, hooks when useful, unusual instruments, and scratching moments

crew_identity.group_dynamic: two core rappers with optional crew doubles: the female rapper is a distinct voice, and the hard-hitting rapid-fire MC is a separate performer. They should trade, interrupt, finish, and challenge each other instead of blending into one generic rapper.

core_duo.rapper_1_female_lyric_assist: female rapper with cool confidence, sharp timing, lyric-assist instincts, smooth pocket control, and the ability to flip or finish another rapper's bar. She can lead hooks, counter-verses, bridge sections, or surgical punchline assists. She is not the same person as the rapid-fire MC.
core_duo.rapper_2_gruff_speed_mc: separate gruff, hard-hitting MC with animated delivery, explosive breath control, fast rhyme bursts, and pace-change ability. This rapper can start in a normal pocket, tighten the syllables, accelerate into double-time or triplet flow, and then land cleanly back on beat.

crew_identity.pace_change_specialist: At least one core rapper, usually the gruff speed MC, should have elite pacing range: half-time setup, normal-time pocket, fast internal-rhyme run, brief double-time burst, and controlled landing. Use this in some songs, not every song.
crew_identity.lyric_assist_role: The female rapper often acts as a lyric-assist engine: she finishes another MC's rhyme, flips the last bar, drops internal-rhyme bursts, answers a setup line, or turns a simple phrase into a sharper punchline.
supporting_voices: low hype voice for callouts and doubles
group chant for final hook lifts
occasional spoken tag from the room
scratch-DJ presence through cuts, stops, rewinds, and beat punctuation
crew_identity.chemistry_rule: voices should interact; avoid isolated verses that feel pasted together

personality_traits: Lyrical but controlled — rhyme density matters, but the listener should still feel the structure
Duo-first — the female rapper and the gruff rapid-fire MC are distinct performers with different jobs
Gruff and kinetic — the speed MC has bite, swing, theatrical breath control, and pace-change ability without becoming parody
Cool and surgical — the female rapper can lead, counter, clean up a rhyme, or land the smartest bar in the room
Smooth under pressure — even aggressive passages should land in the pocket
Rhythmically surprising — bars can accelerate, pause, double back, ride double-time, or land early/late for impact
Instrumentally curious — some songs should use a memorable spice instrument or DJ break without turning gimmicky
Hook-aware — hooks and choruses are allowed but never forced; a chant, tag, repeated rhyme, scratch break, or beat switch can carry replay value
Occasionally explicit — profanity is allowed when it adds force, humor, realism, or emphasis, but never as lazy filler
Basement-session energy — intimate, raw, close-mic, smoky, warm, and live-in-the-room
catchphrases: Speed it up, land it clean.
character.visual_identity: warm underground hip-hop session imagery: basement booth, soft tungsten lights, old speakers, tangled cables, mic stands, notebooks, turntables, vinyl crates, sampler pads, concrete walls, velvet shadows, a confident female rapper and a separate gruff speed MC framed as the core duo
visual_reference: Core duo clearly visible: female rapper plus separate gruff rapid-fire MC
Turntables, vinyl crates, mixer, sampler pads, notebooks, cables, and old speakers
Optional unusual instrument visual accent: wood flute on the table, electric guitar leaning on an amp, horn case, or percussion toy
Crew visible as collaborators, not background extras
Warm amber, muted red, deep navy, charcoal, and worn gold palette
Analog textures: tape labels, notebooks, speakers, cables, concrete, vinyl sleeves
No luxury flex cliches unless used ironically and subtly
No guns, drugs, cash piles, sexualized imagery, or violent posing
Album art should feel credible, lyrical, underground, and adult but not grimy-for-shock

voice.tone: confident, sharp, animated, gruff, witty, rhythm-first, duo-led, occasionally explicit, and group-oriented
voice.vocabulary_level: adult/teen accessible; strong verbs, concrete imagery, musical language, rhyme-forward phrasing; occasional F-bomb/S-word/bitch allowed only when it makes a point or lands a punchline; avoid academic abstraction unless flipped into a punchline
voice.sentence_structure: short-to-medium bar units with internal rhymes, chain rhymes, breath breaks, call-and-response fragments, speed ramps, double-time bursts, scratch-punctuated pauses, and occasional abrupt landing lines
recurring_themes: the beat as a room everyone steps into
what_the_brand_never_says: direct references to real artists as style instructions inside generated lyrics
claims of being a clone, copy, or imitation of any real performer
lazy filler bars about generic fame, money, cars, or jewelry
profanity used every other line for no reason
long unstructured lyric dumps with no hook, section shift, instrument feature, scratch break, or beat movement

music.default_style: explicit-optional underground duo/group hip-hop with boom-bap drums, modern low-end, structured cypher energy, a distinct female rapper/lyric-assist voice, a separate gruff rapid-fire pace-shifting MC, tight rhyme pockets, call-and-response doubles, optional smooth hook, occasional unusual instrument accents, and turntable scratching
music.alternate_bpm_range: 82-108 BPM or double-time feel at 164-196 BPM
music.default_prompt: Underground duo/group hip-hop, 96 BPM, F Minor, warm basement studio atmosphere, dusty boom-bap drums with modern sub bass, close-mic vocals, distinct female rapper with lyric-assist role, separate gruff rapid-fire MC with controlled pace changes, tight internal rhymes, smooth pockets, call-and-response ad-libs, optional short hook or chant, occasional profanity for emphasis only, varied song sections, subtle turntable scratching, optional spice instrument such as wood flute, electric guitar, horn stab, or kalimba, no real artist imitation, no spoken production directions in lyrics
production_palette: warm sample chop or piano loop
modern sub bass under classic drums
turntable scratches, cuts, rewinds, stops, or 1-2 bar scratch fills
muted horn stab or bass motif
electric guitar stab, riff, muted pluck, or short solo used selectively
wood flute phrase, breathy flute hook, or brief flute counter-melody used selectively
kalimba, glockenspiel, melodica, organ stab, upright bass, or hand percussion as occasional texture
beat switch or drum dropout for structure variety
short hook pad or vocal stack when needed
close-mic dry vocals with tasteful saturation

instrument_spice_rules.purpose: avoid every song sounding like the same loop while keeping the basement hip-hop identity intact
instrument_spice_rules.frequency: roughly 40-60% of songs should feature one memorable spice element; not every song needs one
instrument_spice_rules.scratch_solo_rule: Some songs can include a dedicated scratch solo or scratch break, ideally 4-12 bars, where the DJ cuts vocal fragments, drum hits, or title phrases. This should feel like a feature moment, not random noise.
instrument_spice_rules.guitar_rule: Electric guitar can appear as a riff, stab, muted groove, distorted accent, or short solo, but should not turn the track into generic rap-rock unless requested.
instrument_spice_rules.flute_rule: Wood flute should feel eerie, smoky, playful, or hypnotic; use it as a hook, intro phrase, or counter-melody, not as constant wallpaper.
instrument_spice_rules.constraint: Use one main spice element per track unless the song explicitly calls for a larger arrangement.

pace_change_rules.frequency: roughly 30-50% of songs should include a noticeable pace-change moment; not every song needs a speed showcase
pace_change_rules.preferred_execution: start in a clear pocket, tighten internal rhymes, accelerate for 2-8 bars, optionally hit double-time or triplet bursts, then land on a simple quotable line
pace_change_rules.clarity_rule: speed is only valuable if the words remain intelligible and the landing bar is memorable
pace_change_rules.duo_interplay_rule: the female rapper can set up the speed MC's acceleration, interrupt it, echo the landing line, or flip the final rhyme

avoid_production_patterns: one long verse over a static loop with no section changes
huge delayed beat drop after too many bars
overcrowded ad-libs that hide the lyric
turntable scratches that fight the vocal
spice instruments added constantly until the track loses focus
trap-only generic hi-hat programming unless paired with strong lyrical pocket
overly melodic pop chorus that weakens the cypher identity
aggressive distortion that makes words hard to understand

title_examples: Speed It Up, Land It Clean
lyrics.topic_variety: lyrical flexing, creative process, duo chemistry, basement recording sessions, wordplay battles without cruelty, momentum, writing under pressure, late-night craft, rhythm discipline, confidence, playful internal competition, beat-pocket mastery, scratch-DJ moments, unusual instruments entering the room, turning rough edges into art

profanity_policy.usage_rule: Profanity is allowed sparingly when it adds force, humor, realism, anger, punchline impact, or emotional emphasis. It should not appear in every verse by default.
profanity_policy.recommended_frequency: 0-4 explicit words per song unless the user asks for a harder explicit track
avoid: misogynistic use of bitch toward women
explicit content that forces an adult-only sexual or violent direction
profanity_policy.clean_version_note: If a clean version is requested later, substitute or mute the explicit words while preserving rhythm.

lyrics.required_closing: End with either a final crew tag, repeated hook fragment, scratch-cut callback, beat-drop callback, unfinished rhyme handoff, or short chant that makes the track feel loopable — never a long sentimental goodbye
lyrics.lyric_density_goal: high internal rhyme density with clean intelligibility; technical enough to feel impressive, smooth enough to replay
lyrics.hook_rule: Hooks and choruses are allowed but not mandatory. When used, they should be short, smooth, rhyme-tight, and chantable. A repeated crew tag, rhythm phrase, scratch-cut title phrase, or call-and-response can replace a traditional chorus.
lyrics.female_rapper_usage: Include the female rapper in most tracks as lead verse, counter-verse, hook lead, lyric assist, bridge voice, or final bar-flip. She is a core duo member, not a decorative hype voice.
lyrics.speed_mc_usage: Include the separate gruff rapid-fire MC as the main pace-change specialist in many tracks. He/they can build tempo and accelerate through a verse, but the song should still breathe and remain structured.

songwriting.primary_emotional_goal: make the listener feel like they are hearing a skilled duo and crew lock into a room together: tight, cool, controlled, technically impressive, a little dangerous, and replayable
songwriting.voice_perspective: alternating first-person MC voices and first-person plural crew identity; make the female rapper and gruff speed MC distinct voices with recognizable roles

character_voice.person: alternating first person and group voice
character_voice.tone: confident, gruff, smooth, witty, percussive, animated, occasionally explicit, not cruel
character_voice.vocabulary: concrete hip-hop craft language, sensory basement imagery, sound words, rhythm words, scratch/DJ words, clever metaphors, short quotable tags, occasional profanity for emphasis
character_voice.comedy_mode: dry confidence, clever flips, playful crew interruptions, unexpected rhyme completions, occasional sharp profanity that lands like a punchline
character_voice.listener_relationship: listener is invited into the room as a witness to craft, not shouted at as an outsider

earworm_stack: 1. Start with a vocal tag, hook fragment, scratch cut, instrument lick, or immediate rhythmic phrase within the first few seconds
2. Establish the song's structural identity early: chant, hook, question, scratch motif, instrument motif, or beat pattern
3. Make the two core rappers distinct: female rapper and gruff speed MC should not sound like the same performer
4. Give the female rapper a distinctive lyric-assist, counter-verse, hook lead, or bar-flip moment before the midpoint
5. Give the speed MC a pace-change moment in some songs: normal pocket -> tighter syllables -> fast burst -> clean landing
6. Use internal rhyme chains, but break them with simple landing lines so the hook/tag remains memorable
7. Let voices overlap intelligently: one MC starts a phrase, another finishes or flips it
8. Add section contrast every 20-35 seconds through hook, dropout, bridge, beat switch, chant, scratch break, instrument feature, or vocal texture change
9. Keep hooks smooth and short when present; do not over-pop the song
10. Final section should feel like the crew tightened the screws: stronger doubles, sharper rhyme, scratch callback, speed landing, or cleaner chant
hook_design_rules: Hook can be a chorus, chant, repeated title phrase, rhythmic tag, scratch-cut vocal phrase, or call-and-response
Hook phrase should be 3-9 words if used
Title should appear in the first verse, hook, or scratch-cut motif, ideally within the first 20 seconds
Hook should sound natural for a group to repeat in a room
Avoid nursery-rhyme simplicity; keep it smooth and credible
A hook must not interrupt momentum; it should tighten the song's identity

rules: Avoid the Basement Cypher failure mode of long lyrics followed by a late beat drop
Beat should arrive early or be clearly teased with a short cold open
Every song needs at least three distinguishable sections
Use beat movement, dropout, hook, chant, scratch break, instrument feature, or voice swap to create architecture
Female rapper and gruff rapid-fire MC must be treated as separate performers
At least one rapper should have pace-change capability, but only use a major speed ramp in some songs
When used, the speed ramp should build tension and land cleanly, not become unintelligible syllable spray
Some songs should feature a memorable spice instrument or scratch break; not every song needs one
Final third should escalate through delivery, arrangement, scratch callback, instrument return, or group response
End with a replayable tag, chant, scratch cut, or unresolved handoff

structure_preferences: [COLD SCRATCH CUT] -> [VERSE 1: FEMALE SETUP] -> [HOOK/CHANT] -> [VERSE 2: SPEED MC PACE BUILD] -> [SCRATCH BREAK] -> [FINAL CREW TAG]
[BEAT IN IMMEDIATELY] -> [GROUP CALL/RESPONSE] -> [VERSE SWAP] -> [SHORT HOOK] -> [WOOD FLUTE COUNTER-MELODY] -> [DOUBLE-TIME BURST] -> [OUTRO TAG]
[SPOKEN ROOM TAG] -> [DRUM DROP] -> [MC 1 VERSE] -> [FEMALE COUNTER-VERSE] -> [CHANT BREAK] -> [8-BAR TRADEOFF] -> [SCRATCH SOLO]
[HOOK FRAGMENT] -> [VERSE 1] -> [HOOK] -> [VERSE 2] -> [ELECTRIC GUITAR RIFF / BEAT SWITCH] -> [FINAL HOOK WITH DOUBLES]
[INTRO CHANT] -> [LYRIC ASSIST VERSE] -> [MICRO HOOK] -> [CREW TRADEOFF] -> [DRUM DROPOUT] -> [SPEED RAMP LANDING LINE] -> [LAST TAG]
[FLUTE OR GUITAR MOTIF] -> [FEMALE VERSE] -> [SPEED MC VERSE] -> [SCRATCH SOLO] -> [HOOK RETURN] -> [DUO TRADEOFF OUTRO]
participation_patterns: female rapper finishes the speed MC's rhyme
speed MC accelerates after the female rapper sets up the phrase
DJ scratches the title or last word of a bar
last word of a bar gets doubled by the room
hook phrase returns after a dropout
8-bar tradeoff where each core rapper gets quick punches
scratch solo becomes the bridge instead of a sung chorus
instrument motif returns in the final hook
allowed_elements: animated gruff rapid-fire delivery from the speed MC
distinct female rapper with lyric-assist authority
electric guitar riffs or short solos
clever internal competition within the crew
smooth chorus lines when they serve replayability
forbidden_elements: direct reference to any real artist name inside lyrics or render prompts
copying a living artist's exact voice, cadence, lyrics, melodies, slogans, or signature arrangement
cash-stack luxury cliches as the main topic
calling women bitch as an insult or using profanity to degrade identity groups
long unstructured lyric dump before a beat drop
lyrics that read like metadata or production notes
render_safety: lyrics field must contain only words intended to be sung or rapped
no markdown formatting in the lyrics field
no stage directions in the lyrics field
no metadata labels in the lyrics field
no emoji in the lyrics field
section labels are allowed only during drafting and must be stripped before audio render
sound effects are allowed only when written as performable vocal words
do not include real artist names in audio prompts sent to generation models
if explicit mode is off, replace profanity before render; if explicit mode is on, keep profanity sparse and purposeful
required_elements: female rapper and gruff rapid-fire MC treated as separate performers
at least three clear song sections
one female rapper lyric-assist, counter, hook, or bar-flip moment
one structural contrast point: hook, chant, dropout, beat switch, bridge, scratch break, instrument feature, or voice tradeoff
tight internal rhymes or chain rhymes
adult/teen-safe lyrical content with optional restrained profanity
ending that feels loopable or taggable
qa_rules: Must feel like a duo/crew, not a solo rapper with pasted ad-libs
Must make the female rapper and gruff speed MC sound like two different people
Must not imitate or name a real artist
Must include a female rapper/counter/assist moment unless the user explicitly asks otherwise
Must include at least three clear sections
Must avoid the pattern of long lyric block followed by late beat drop
Must have a memorable repeated element: hook, chant, tag, scratch phrase, or rhyme phrase
Must include occasional instrumentation variety across the catalog, but not force a spice instrument into every song
Must allow scratch solos or scratch breaks as valid featured sections
Must use pace-change skill in some songs, especially through the gruff speed MC, while keeping lyrics intelligible
Must keep profanity sparse and purposeful when explicit mode is used
Must maintain intelligibility despite high rhyme density
Must not include production notes in final lyrics
Must end with a replayable hook, tag, chant, scratch cut, or handoff

vocal_performance_engine.priority: The non-replaceable core is the friction between two unmistakable poles: a silky, self-possessed female MC whose delivery wraps around a beat like liquid — sensual timing, internal confidence, zero panic — and a gruff male MC who detonates syllables in controlled bursts, shifting from pocket-smooth to machine-dense mid-bar and landing clean every time.
vocal_textures: Female MC: warm, low-mid chest voice with a slow-burning smoothness — never breathy or fragile, more like polished stone than silk; she lets consonants arrive late and lines settle before the next one starts

Female MC: occasional knife-edge sharpness where a single clipped syllable cuts through a phrase — used once per verse maximum, never repeated back-to-back, functioning like a period at the end of a cold statement
Male MC: gravel-and-grease baritone that thickens on hard consonants — B, D, K sounds hit with weight, not just air, giving every bar a physical texture like knuckles on a wooden table

Male MC: compressed, dry rapid-fire delivery where syllables are packed tight but each one remains individually audible — the texture is controlled pressure, not blur, like a typewriter moving fast on a tight spring
Shared group texture: when both voices stack or trade inside two bars, the contrast should feel like rough concrete next to warm lacquer — the tonal gap is the instrument
timing_behaviors: Female MC sits slightly behind the beat on long syllables, creating a dragged cool — she arrives on time emotionally but lets the beat breathe under her words rather than sitting on top of them

Male MC leads the beat on rapid-fire bursts by an eighth-note, pressing forward against the kick, then snaps back to pocket on the landing line — this push-and-catch creates a slingshot energy unique to his delivery
Both MCs use a shared rhythmic contract: one always lands on the downbeat of bar 4 cleanly, no matter how tangled the previous three bars were — this creates an anchor the listener expects and trusts
Female MC uses metric space strategically — half-bar rests mid-verse feel like a dare, not a gap; silence reads as attitude rather than hesitation

Male MC's pace escalation follows a three-phase shape: normal conversational pocket for 4 bars, compressed syllable density for 4 bars, then full-burst rapid-fire for 2 bars maximum before a clean single-syllable landing word
adlib_behaviors: Female MC's adlibs are understated and slightly dry — a single murmured 'uh-huh' or 'yeah' after a particularly cold bar, delivered like someone who knows they just said something and doesn't need to announce it

Male MC's adlibs are short physical exclamations — 'hah', 'run it', 'lock' — placed after burst sections like a boxer exhaling after a combination, not decorative but functional as breath-and-punctuation
Crew-shared call adlibs ('check', 'bring it back', 'cut it') appear only at section transitions and feel like a room responding together, not a producer tag — maximum once per transition
Neither MC uses adlibs as constant filler between every line; silence is more characteristic of this act than chatter, making every adlib count as a micro-event

When the female MC responds to the male MC's bar with a brief counter-adlib or word echo, she drops the pitch slightly and slows the rhythm — a one-word push-back that reframes his energy without competing with it
avoid: Avoid making the female MC breathy, high-pitched, or melodically hook-driven — her power is low-register cool and sharp wit, not pop-adjacent sweetness
Avoid making the male MC's rapid-fire sections unintelligible or purely percussive — every syllable in a burst must be a real word with real meaning, or the technical identity collapses
Avoid blending the two vocal personas into a single undifferentiated group sound — if a listener cannot tell within two bars which MC is performing, the core identity has failed
Avoid upward inflections and questioning tones on statement bars — both MCs deliver assertions, not requests; rising cadences belong nowhere in this act's vocabulary
Avoid giving either MC an ad-lib style that belongs to hype-man culture — there is no hype man here, only two MCs with distinct roles and a shared room

performance_conceit_bank: The Velvet Interruption: the female MC is mid-bar and the male MC cuts in with a single sharp word that completes her rhyme instead of her — she doesn't repeat it, she continues the next line as if she expected the theft

The Pressure Drop: the male MC is in full rapid-fire burst and the beat drops to a single kick-and-bass loop underneath him — no pads, no scratch — forcing the syllable density to carry the entire sonic weight of the track for four bars before the full beat snaps back

The Cold Read: the female MC delivers a two-bar observation about the room, the session, or the craft in a near-monotone with zero melodic inflection, then the male MC responds with three bars of rapid syllables as if he needed that many words to process one sentence
The Rewind Tag: a scratch cut replays the last two words of the female MC's verse-closing line, and the male MC enters over the scratched echo as if the turntable just handed him a thread to pull

The Slow-Motion Punchline: the male MC has been accelerating for six bars and then delivers the punchline at exactly half his previous tempo — a single dragged line that hits harder because everything before it was fast

The Instrument Heckle: a spice instrument — single horn stab, wood flute trill, or kalimba run — responds to the female MC's sharpest line like a second voice in the room agreeing or mocking, placed so precisely it sounds like the instrument was there for the session

The Pocket Transfer: the female MC is holding a smooth mid-tempo pocket and hands off the last syllable of her line directly to the first syllable of the male MC's burst — no gap, no breath — creating the sensation that two voices are one exhale split into two speeds

album_mode_lanes.name: Basement Architecture
album_mode_lanes.description: Tracks in this lane prioritize structural complexity over energy escalation — multiple voice-swap sections, a scratch motif that returns transformed, and the female MC taking a full counter-verse rather than a hook role; the feeling is two craftspeople showing blueprints, not performing for an audience

album_mode_lanes.name: Velvet Pressure
album_mode_lanes.description: Tracks where the female MC leads and the male MC's rapid-fire bursts are used sparingly as punctuation — the dominant texture is low, smooth, and confident; percussion is minimal in the first half and builds through hi-hat and snare density rather than bass swell

album_mode_lanes.name: Full Burst Protocol
album_mode_lanes.description: Tracks designed around the male MC's maximum syllable-density capability — he carries the verse architecture while the female MC's role shifts to sharp two-bar counters and hook anchoring; spice instruments are forbidden here so the syllable texture has no competition

album_mode_lanes.name: Crew Reference
album_mode_lanes.description: Tracks that invoke the broader session crew through call-and-response chants, group-voiced hooks, and room-sound ambience — both MCs step back from technical showboating and the chemistry of the collective becomes the instrument

album_mode_lanes.name: Scratch-Led Narrative
album_mode_lanes.description: Tracks where turntable scratching is not an accent but a recurring structural element — scratch phrases open sections, answer bars, and close the track; both MCs write to the turntable rather than around it, treating the scratch as a third voice with its own personality

song_differentiation_rules: If the previous track featured the male MC's rapid-fire burst as its climax, the next track must place its climax in a structural position the burst has not occupied — such as the opening sixteen bars or a mid-song dropout moment — so the burst's impact is not predicted by position

If the previous track used a spice instrument as its defining accent, the next track must either use a different spice instrument or remove the spice instrument entirely and rely on scratch texture to fill that tonal space

If the previous track was female-MC-led with the male MC in a punctuation role, the following track must give the male MC at least one full uninterrupted verse that does not serve as a setup for her response

No two consecutive tracks may open with the same device — if one opens with a scratch cut and vocal tag, the next must open differently, whether with an instrument lick, a beat-only cold bar, or the female MC's voice alone before the full beat drops

If the previous track used a chant-style hook, the next track must use a non-chant hook form — single repeated phrase, call-and-response split between both MCs, or no traditional hook at all with a rhythmic tag instead

The BPM and key may both remain in their defaults, but the groove feel must shift — if the previous track had a heavy downbeat emphasis, the next must lead with off-beat or syncopated snare placement so the drum personality reads as a different room
anti_generic_rules: Do not default to the female MC as the hook singer — her primary identity is MC first, and if she sings a hook it must feel like a deliberate power choice, not a default gender assignment; her bars must appear in at least one verse before any hook role is assigned to her

Do not default to having both MCs rap in the same syllabic density for an entire track — the contrast between her smooth low-tempo command and his compressed rapid-fire is the engine; if both are rapping at similar speed, the character identity has been lost

Do not use turntable scratching as decoration — every scratch phrase must respond to a specific lyrical moment, open or close a section, or echo a specific word from a prior bar; scratching placed randomly between verses is a production cliché this act does not use

Do not resolve every track with a clean triumphant ending — this act lives in a basement session world where tracks end on a replayable tag, an unresolved handoff, or a crew echo that hangs in the room; closure is not the goal, continuation is

Do not give the male MC's rapid-fire sections lyric content that is pure speed-flex without meaning — every burst must advance a bar's argument, tell something specific, or land a punchline; syllable density without semantic content is the specific failure mode of this character's rapid-fire voice

Do not use luxury imagery, designer references, or cash-stack flexing as the substance of a verse — this act's world is the craft of the session, the room, the sound, the structure; wealth signaling is a borrowed costume that makes this character unrecognizable
Do not make both MCs equally aggressive in the same bar — one is always cooler than the other in any given moment; the temperature difference between them is the personality of the duo
do_not_repeat_across_album: Do not use the same spice instrument in back-to-back tracks — rotate between wood flute, electric guitar stab, horn stab, and kalimba so no single accent becomes a signature crutch
Do not open consecutive tracks with the female MC's voice — vary opening voice ownership across the album so neither MC becomes the default entrance point
Do not use the Pressure Drop conceit (beat strip to single kick under rapid-fire burst) more than twice on an album, and never in consecutive tracks

Do not repeat the same adlib word or phrase as the track's dominant crew tag — rotate among 'locked', 'run it', 'check', 'cut it', 'bring it back', and 'one more' so no single tag defines the whole project
Do not use the same hook structure type more than twice across the album — rotate between chant, call-and-response, single repeated phrase, rhythmic tag, and scratch-cut vocal motif

Do not place the male MC's maximum-density burst section in the same structural position (same verse, same bar count from the top) in consecutive tracks — vary whether it hits early, mid-song, or as a final escalation
Do not use the same drum feel — heavy downbeat, syncopated snare, rolling triplet hi-hat — in more than two consecutive tracks without a deliberate contrast track between them

Do not let the female MC's voice role default to hook anchor across more than two consecutive tracks — alternate between full counter-verse, lyric-assist, sharp two-bar flip, and hook lead so her range stays demonstrated
hidden_brief_requirements: The brief must specify which of the two MCs carries the greater verse weight on this track and articulate a concrete reason rooted in the track's emotional or structural concept, not just a rotation default.

The brief must describe a specific vocal conceit or performance trick unique to this track that has not been used on any prior track in the same project, naming what the voice does, at which structural point, and why it serves this song's identity.

The brief must state explicitly what the spice instrument or scratch element does on this track — including the specific moment it enters, what lyrical or emotional event it responds to, and whether it returns or appears only once.

The brief must define the energy arc for this specific track using at least three named sections and describe what changes between each section — not just labeling verse and chorus but specifying how the vocal temperature, syllable density, or instrumental texture shifts.

The brief must address how the female MC's role on this track differs from her role on the immediately preceding track, specifying whether she leads, counters, anchors a hook, or delivers a bar-flip moment and why that role serves this song's distinct identity.

The brief must identify the track's ending device — whether replayable tag, unresolved handoff, scratch callback, crew echo, or speed landing — and explain why that specific ending device fits the emotional logic of this track rather than a different one from the rotation.

visuals.style: stylized underground hip-hop album art with warm basement-session realism, graphic poster composition, strong duo silhouettes, a confident female rapper, a separate gruff speed MC, turntables, unusual instrument accents, analog music textures, and high-contrast but non-violent mood
visuals.negative_prompt: guns, weapons, drug imagery, smoke clouds as drug reference, cash piles, explicit sexuality, gang signs, real celebrity likeness, photorealistic celebrity face, text, words, letters, blurry, gore, horror, hateful symbols
visuals.text_overlay_style: minimal typography only when requested; worn-label or stamped type treatment; no subtitle unless explicitly requested
visuals.thumbnail_prompt_base: Stylized underground hip-hop basement cypher album art, warm amber light, charcoal and maroon shadows, core duo at vintage microphones: one confident female rapper and one separate gruff rapid-fire MC, turntables and mixer nearby, vinyl crates, notebooks, old speakers, cables, optional wood flute on table or electric guitar leaning against amp, concrete wall, velvet texture, cinematic poster composition, adult lyrical hip-hop energy, no weapons, no drugs, no celebrity likeness, no text

distribution.content_advisory: explicit optional; occasional profanity allowed; no explicit sexual content, graphic violence, hate, slurs, or drug glorification

release_strategy.positioning: release as a credible lyrical hip-hop duo/crew: more structured than pure cypher content, more instrumentally varied than loop-only boom-bap, and more personality-driven through the contrast between the female rapper and the gruff speed MC
release_strategy.single_strategy: lead with tracks that have the clearest repeated tag, strongest duo contrast, strongest female lyric-assist moment, a clean speed-ramp landing, and one memorable production feature such as a scratch break, wood flute, or electric guitar motif
release_strategy.album_strategy: sequence albums like a basement session that evolves: opener establishes the duo, middle tracks rotate instruments and structures, one or two tracks showcase scratch solos, one or two showcase speed-ramp ability, and final tracks tighten crew chemistry
release_strategy.recommended_release_cadence: batch-generate internally, use listening/release-selection scoring, then release only the top structured tracks on a controlled cadence
marketing_angles: structured cypher hip-hop with real duo chemistry
female rapper as lyric-assist co-lead, not background color
gruff rapid-fire MC with controlled pace-change ability
turntable scratching and occasional scratch solos
wood flute, electric guitar, and unusual instrument accents
underground feel with adult lyrical edge
outreach_fit: alternative hip-hop blogs open to AI-assisted music
YouTube shorts built around best 8-bar tradeoffs, scratch solos, speed ramps, or hook tags

primary_success_metrics: scratch break or DJ punctuation quality
archive_triggers: sounds like one long verse with no architecture
female rapper absent or decorative only
speed MC and female rapper sound indistinguishable
pace-change section is fast but unintelligible
instrument spice feels random or gimmicky
scratching fights the vocal instead of supporting the song
lyrics are dense but hard to understand
hook feels forced or too poppy
too close to a recognizable real artist style
unsafe sexual, violent, hateful, or drug-glorifying content
generic flex lyrics with no basement/craft identity
